articles.title: Re-triage of severely injured patients in no-fault vs. at-fault states.
articles.authors: Visenio MR, Thivalapill N, Silver CM, Sullivan GA, Thomas AC, Reddy S, et al.
articles.population: Severely injured Medicare patients (injury severity score > 15) from motor-vehicle collisions and falls
articles.sample_size: 6447 Medicare patients across 345 hospitals in four US states
articles.outcomes: Same-day inter-hospital transfer to a high-level trauma centre (re-triage)
articles.take_home: Patients severely injured in car crashes in no-fault insurance states were less likely to be urgently transferred to specialist trauma centres compared to those in at-fault states, suggesting insurance laws may affect how hospitals manage severely injured patients.

articles.title: Stacked-Cone Constructs for Extensive Tibial and/or Femoral Bone Loss in Complex Primary and Revision TKA: A Multicenter Analysis of 84 Cases.
articles.authors: Kumaran P, Telang SS, Culler M, Palmer RC, Longjohn DB, Oakes DA, et al.
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary or revision total knee arthroplasty with extensive tibial and/or femoral bone loss
articles.outcomes: 5-year implant survivorship free from all-cause reoperation, all-cause revision, and stacked-cone construct revision for aseptic loosening
articles.take_home: Stacked-cone constructs used to address severe bone loss in knee replacement surgery had good long-term survival without loosening, but patients needed additional operations or revision surgery fairly often, which reflects how complex these cases are.

articles.title: Interpret with caution: randomized controlled trials comparing cemented versus press-fit hemiarthroplasty in geriatric patients with intracapsular hip fractures are statistically fragile.
articles.authors: Quinn M, Lou M, Morrissey P, Burstein E, Milner J, Evans AR, et al.
articles.study_type: Systematic review of randomized controlled trials
articles.population: Geriatric patients with intracapsular hip fractures treated with hemiarthroplasty
articles.outcomes: Mortality, periprosthetic fractures, dislocations, revisions, and other postoperative complications
articles.take_home: Many of the trial results comparing cemented and press-fit hip implants are statistically fragile, meaning small changes in patient outcomes could reverse the reported findings. Results on mortality, fractures, dislocations and revisions should be interpreted cautiously because high loss-to-follow-up rates affect the reliability of these comparisons.

articles.title: The impact of duration of infection on outcome of debridement, antimicrobial treatment and implant retention in fracture-related infections of the lower leg: A multicentre retrospective cohort study.
articles.authors: Sliepen J, Buijs MAS, Wouthuyzen-Bakker M, Depypere M, Rentenaar RJ, De Vries JPPM, et al.
articles.population: Adults over 18 years with fracture-related infections of the lower leg treated with debridement, antibiotics and implant retention within 12 weeks of initial fracture fixation
articles.outcomes: Treatment success defined as implant retention, infection eradication, fracture healing and limb preservation. Success rate 57% at median follow-up of 33.6 months
articles.take_home: The time between initial fracture fixation and infection treatment surgery did not significantly affect outcomes up to nine weeks, meaning doctors may consider keeping the implant in place for infection treatment even if the infection appears later, provided other surgical factors are suitable. However, overall success was limited at 57%.

articles.title: Geriatric tibial plateau fractures: A multicenter analysis of complications and treatment strategies.
articles.authors: Martin P, Springer F, Klaut M, Bormann M, Neidlein C, Furmetz J, et al.
articles.population: Patients aged 70 years and over with surgically treated intra-articular tibial plateau fractures
articles.outcomes: Postoperative complications including wound healing disorders, deformities, infections, and revision surgery rates; association between fracture type, surgical approach, and complications
articles.take_home: About one quarter of older patients with tibial plateau fractures experienced complications after surgery, with more complex fracture types and longer operations carrying higher risk. The type and complexity of the fracture mattered more than patients' age or other health conditions in predicting problems.

articles.title: PRIoritisation of orthopaedic resources and interventions in trauma (PRIORI-T): A modified delphi consensus study.
articles.authors: Kotze JD, Amod M, Breedt C, Anley C, Ferreira N, Maimin D, et al.
articles.population: South African public-sector orthopaedic clinicians
articles.outcomes: Consensus on factors for prioritising orthopaedic surgery in resource-constrained settings, including six patient-specific factors, all six injury-specific factors, and two red-flag conditions
articles.take_home: Clinicians have agreed on key factors that should guide decisions about which patients need orthopaedic surgery first when resources are limited, such as soft-tissue condition and blood vessel damage. The study provides a framework to guide these decisions fairly, but more testing is needed before it can be used in hospitals.

articles.title: Risk factors for revision amputation and infection after definitive closure of traumatic amputations: a retrospective cohort study at a Level I academic trauma center.
articles.authors: Kakalecik J, Brnjos K, Habib A, Vasilopoulos T, Hagen JE, Krupko TA
articles.population: Patients with traumatic lower extremity amputation or injuries requiring amputation at a level one trauma centre
articles.outcomes: Risk factors for revision amputation and postoperative infection; amputation level; ambulation rates and need for assistive devices
articles.take_home: Patients who needed extra surgery after their initial amputation closure, or who had a higher body mass index, were more likely to need further amputation. Keeping the amputation below the knee, when possible, led to better walking ability and less need for walking aids.

articles.title: Periprosthetic fracture following hip resurfacing arthroplasty: treatment guidance using the 'SAVE' classification system.
articles.authors: Al-Jabri T, Mirdad RS, Wong JM, Shankar S, Masterson S, Giannoudis PV
articles.study_type: Classification system development and review article
articles.population: Patients with periprosthetic fractures following hip resurfacing arthroplasty
articles.outcomes: Development of the SAVE classification system (Stability, Anatomical location, Viability, Environment) to guide treatment decisions between implant preservation and revision arthroplasty
articles.take_home: Fractures around hip resurfacing implants need a different approach to standard hip fracture classifications because of how the implants are designed. The new SAVE classification system helps doctors decide whether to repair or replace the implant by considering four key factors: how stable the implant is, where the fracture is, whether the bone is still getting blood supply, and the overall tissue condition.

articles.title: Distal femur fractures: a comparison of hybrid and non-hybrid fixation of standard locking plates.
articles.authors: Plumarom Y, Singsuwit T, Marsh L, Phruetthiphat OA
articles.population: Patients with distal femur fractures treated with locking plate fixation
articles.sample_size: 90 patients with 90 fractures (26 hybrid fixation, 64 non-hybrid fixation)
articles.outcomes: Fracture healing progression measured by modified Radiological Union Scale for Tibia score at 6, 12, and 24 weeks; union rates and complications at final follow-up
articles.take_home: Hybrid fixation of locking plates showed faster early bone healing signs at 6 to 24 weeks compared with non-hybrid fixation. However, both approaches achieved similar final healing rates (100% and 97% respectively) and similar complication rates, suggesting both methods work equally well in the long term.

articles.title: Association between intraoperative fluoroscopy for anterior approach total hip arthroplasty and short-term revision risk: an analysis based on 49,878 cases from the Dutch Arthroplasty Register.
articles.authors: Vink MC, Zijlstra WP, Van Steenbergen LN, Van Veen PA, Doorn PF, Kuiper JWP, et al.
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary total hip replacement via direct anterior approach in the Netherlands
articles.outcomes: Short-term revision risk (up to 2.5 years), revision rates due to malalignment and periprosthetic fractures
articles.take_home: Using intraoperative fluoroscopy during hip replacement via the direct anterior approach was associated with a slightly lower risk of needing revision surgery within 2.5 years compared to conventional surgery without fluoroscopy, though overall revision rates were low in both groups and the clinical difference was small.

articles.title: An Acta Orthopaedica educational article: Choosing treatment with an elderly patient having a distal radius fracture.
articles.authors: Landgren M, Navarro CM, Karjalainen T, Thorninger R, Hassellund S, Troest RW, et al.
articles.population: Elderly adults with distal radius fractures
articles.outcomes: Treatment outcomes comparing non-surgical and surgical management; complication rates; radiological assessment; osteoporosis screening findings
articles.take_home: There is limited high-quality evidence comparing non-surgical casting with surgery for elderly patients with wrist fractures, and treatment choice should be individualised based on the patient's functional needs and personal circumstances rather than using a one-size-fits-all approach.

articles.title: Comparable outcomes for non-operative intracranial hemorrhage at Level III vs Level I/II trauma centers.
articles.authors: Lord S, Arda Y, Karikis I, DeWane MP, Paranjape CN, Ng-Kamstra JS, et al.
articles.study_type: Retrospective comparative cohort study using national trauma registry data
articles.population: Adults with isolated, closed blunt intracranial haemorrhage managed without surgery
articles.sample_size: 24,132 patients (22,846 at Level I/II centres; 1,286 at Level III centres)
articles.outcomes: Composite in-hospital adverse event counts, mortality, ICU admission rates, unplanned ICU transfers, and time to discharge
articles.take_home: Carefully selected patients with non-operative intracranial bleeding can be safely managed at smaller Level III trauma centres without higher complication rates or need for surgery. Mortality was actually lower at Level III centres, suggesting appropriate patient selection rather than centre level determines outcomes.

articles.title: Inclusion and acknowledgment of social determinants of health in trauma and injury research using national databases: A scoping review.
articles.authors: Kovacs MS, Paxton RJ, Wang JJ, Davis DR, Stoecker AK, Cucher DJ
articles.population: Research papers using national injury databases (ACS TQIP, NEISS, H-CUP NEDS)
articles.sample_size: 315 papers reviewed across four journals per database
articles.outcomes: Percentage of papers including or acknowledging social determinants of health (socioeconomic status, physical environment, racism, discrimination, gender identity)
articles.take_home: Most trauma and injury research using national databases does not measure or discuss social determinants of health, though acknowledgement varies by database used (from 4.3% to 83.3% of studies). This gap is important because social factors significantly affect patients' health status and recovery from injury.

articles.title: Comparing the analgesic utility & safety of erector spinae plane block versus thoracic epidural for multiple rib fracture trauma: a retrospective cohort analysis.
articles.authors: Merchant N, MacLean DJ, Vincze SR, Chhabra J, Walker A, Finkel K
articles.population: Adult patients with multiple rib fracture trauma
articles.sample_size: 324 patients (188 thoracic epidural block, 136 erector spinae plane block)
articles.outcomes: Pain scores at rest and with activity over five days, opioid consumption, length of stay, timing of blood clot prevention medication, need for blood pressure support, complications, and mortality
articles.take_home: Both nerve block types provided similar pain relief for multiple rib fractures over five days. The erector spinae plane block allowed patients to receive blood clot prevention medication more quickly and reliably without interruption, suggesting it may be safer than thoracic epidural block.

articles.title: Drug screening in patients with depressed GCS: An evaluation of current practice and potential bias.
articles.authors: Serio F, Jenkins PD, Barton C, Loss L, Tinoco-Garcia L, Kiraly L, et al.
articles.population: Adult trauma patients with depressed Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS ≤12)
articles.outcomes: Urine drug screening (UDS) utilisation rates, positive test results, referral for screening brief intervention and referral to treatment (SBIRT)
articles.take_home: Only 39% of trauma patients with depressed consciousness received drug screening, although two-thirds of those tested were positive. Older patients and those discharged from emergency department were less likely to be screened, suggesting that standardised screening could improve substance use detection and treatment.

articles.title: A retrospective cohort study on the clinical effectiveness of the modified masquelet technique with platelet-rich plasma for large infected bone defects.
articles.authors: Yang Z, Li X, Sun H, Guan H, Fang H, Zhao P, et al.
articles.population: Patients with large post-traumatic infected bone defects larger than 6 cm
articles.outcomes: Bone healing time (mean 5.6 months), union rate (100%), pain reduction (VAS 7.7 to 2.5), functional improvement (LEFS, DASH, SF-36), Paley score (90.6% excellent-to-good), complications (18.8% overall)
articles.take_home: All 32 patients with large infected bone defects achieved bone union using the modified Masquelet technique combined with platelet-rich plasma, with significant improvements in pain, limb function and quality of life. The approach was generally safe with low complication rates and no cases of nonunion or deep infection.

articles.title: Clinical and Radiographic Outcomes at a Mean of 7 Years Following Primary Transfibular Total Ankle Arthroplasty in Younger and Older Patients.
articles.authors: Schafer KA, Day J, Motsay M, Zhang H, Zhang Z, Schon LC
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary transfibular total ankle arthroplasty, stratified by age (younger than 55 years and 55 years or older)
articles.sample_size: 251 ankles in 236 patients (72 ankles in younger group, 179 ankles in older group)
articles.outcomes: Implant survivorship, patient-reported outcome measures (SF-12, Ankle Osteoarthritis Scale, pain scores), radiographic alignment, range of motion, periprosthetic cysts, adverse events, and reoperations at mean 7 years follow-up
articles.take_home: Transfibular total ankle replacement produced similar good midterm results in both younger (average age 45) and older (average age 66) patients, with no significant differences in complications or need for revision surgery. This suggests the procedure may be a suitable option for end-stage ankle arthritis across a wide range of ages.

articles.title: External fixators versus titanium elastic nailing for distal diaphyseal-metaphyseal junction tibial fractures: A finite element biomechanical analysis.
articles.authors: Wen Y, Xu C, Wang Q, Zhu D, Song B, Feng W
articles.study_type: Finite element biomechanical analysis using computer modelling
articles.population: Tibial fracture models based on imaging data from an 11-year-old child
articles.sample_size: Computer models of 2 fracture types (transverse and oblique)
articles.outcomes: Rotational stiffness, bending stiffness, and axial stiffness of external fixators versus titanium elastic nails
articles.take_home: External fixators provided better rotational and bending stability than titanium elastic nails in both fracture types, which may be important for fractures at the lower part of the shin bone where weight-bearing restrictions are used after surgery.

articles.title: Utilization of a Utility Plate in the Treatment of Periprosthetic Humeral Shaft Fractures Around a Shoulder Arthroplasty.
articles.authors: Friedman LGM, Thorman A, Mehta S, Horneff JG
articles.population: Patients with periprosthetic humeral shaft fractures around a shoulder arthroplasty
articles.outcomes: Fixation stability using a utility plate with fixed angle screws
articles.take_home: A utility plate that allows screws to be placed at different angles can provide secure fixation of fractures around the shoulder joint when the implant is stable and can be kept in place.

articles.title: Surgical Treatment of an Acetabular Fracture Using a Novel Intrapelvic Acetabular System.
articles.authors: Mader M, Kamin K, Walther EJ, Herold J, Schaser KD
articles.population: Adult patient with complex acetabular fracture
articles.outcomes: Anatomic reduction maintained, complete fracture healing, return to pre-injury function at 6 months
articles.take_home: A single patient with a complex acetabular fracture treated with a new three-dimensional anatomically shaped plate achieved good fracture healing and functional recovery at 6-month follow-up. This case demonstrates the technique but is from one patient only, so more research is needed to confirm the benefits of this new plate system.
